questions.question:
Some code optimizations are carried out on the intermediate code because
A. They enhance the portability of the compiler to the target processor
B. Program analysis is more accurate on intermediate code than on machine code
C. The information from dataflow analysis cannot otherwise be used for optimization
D. The information from the front end cannot otherwise be used for optimization
questions.answer: A. They enhance the portability of the compiler to the target processor
questions.to_find: The reason why some code optimizations are performed on intermediate code rather than machine code

understand.plain: A compiler translates source code first into an intermediate representation (IR) and then into machine code. Some optimizations are applied at the IR stage rather than at the machine code stage. The question asks why this choice is made. The key reason is that intermediate code is machine-independent, so optimizations done there work across different target processors, enhancing portability.

keywords.explain: A machine-independent representation of the program produced between the front end and back end of a compiler (e.g., three-address code, quadruples).
Transforming code to improve performance (speed, space) without changing its semantics.
keywords.example: Replacing x*2 with x<<1 for speed

keywords.explain: The ability of software (or a compiler) to work correctly on different hardware/target processors with minimal change.
keywords.example: An IR-level optimizer works for x86, ARM, RISC-V without rewriting for each

understand.visual_alt: Compiler pipeline: Source -> Front End -> Intermediate Code -> Optimizer -> Back End -> Machine Code. Optimization at IR stage is machine-independent.

given.aim: Identify the correct reason for performing optimizations on intermediate code.

terms.meaning: The compiler phase that improves the IR before code generation
terms.example: Dead code elimination on three-address code
terms.connects: Machine-independence here means the optimizer does not need to be rewritten per target processor

terms.meaning: IR-level changes do not depend on a specific CPU's instruction set
terms.example: Constant folding on IR works identically for x86 and ARM
terms.connects: Performing optimization here enhances compiler portability

terms.meaning: Analysis that computes properties of data (reaching definitions, live variables) across program flow
terms.example: Determining which variables are live at each point
terms.connects: Dataflow analysis can be used at the IR level; options C and D are distractors

given.plan: Evaluate each option: A is correct because optimizing at the IR level makes the optimizer reusable across different target machines, directly enhancing portability. B is incorrect — accuracy is not the reason. C and D are incorrect because dataflow and front-end information CAN be used at the machine code level too; portability is the primary architectural motivation.

steps.title: Understand the compiler architecture
steps.apply: A compiler has a front end (parsing, semantic analysis, IR generation) and a back end (code generation for a specific target). The intermediate representation (IR) sits between them and is machine-independent.
steps.note: Because IR is machine-independent, any optimization done at this level does not need to be redone for each target processor.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply:
A: Correct — optimizations on IR are machine-independent, so they work for any target processor, enhancing portability of the compiler.
B: Incorrect — program analysis accuracy is not the reason; in fact, machine code analysis can be equally or more accurate in some cases.
C: Incorrect — dataflow analysis results CAN be used on machine code as well.
D: Incorrect — front-end information can also be used for machine-code optimization.
steps.note: The standard textbook reason (Aho, Lam, Sethi, Ullman) for machine-independent optimization at IR level is precisely portability: the same optimizer component is reused for all target processors.

solution.result: A. They enhance the portability of the compiler to the target processor

questions.formula_note: Machine-independent code optimization: optimizations performed on intermediate representation improve compiler portability across different target processors.

questions.question:
Which one of the following is FALSE?
A. A basic block is a sequence of instructions where control enters the sequence at the beginning and exits at the end.
B. Available expression analysis can be used for common subexpression elimination.
C. Live variable analysis can be used for dead code elimination.
D. z = (4 + 5) => z = 20 is an example of common subexpression elimination.
questions.answer: D. z = (4 + 5) => z = 20 is an example of common subexpression elimination.
questions.to_find: The FALSE statement among the given options about code optimization concepts

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of compiler optimization terminology. Three of the four statements correctly describe optimization concepts. One statement misidentifies a type of optimization: computing 4+5 at compile time to get 9 (not 20) is called constant folding, not common subexpression elimination. Also note 4+5=9, not 20.

keywords.explain: An optimization where an expression that has already been computed and whose operands have not changed is replaced by the previously computed value, avoiding redundant computation.
keywords.example: If a+b is computed twice with no intervening changes, the second occurrence is replaced by the stored result.

keywords.explain: An optimization where constant expressions (expressions with all-constant operands) are evaluated at compile time rather than at runtime.
keywords.example: 4 + 5 is replaced by 9 at compile time.

keywords.explain: A dataflow analysis that determines, at each program point, which variables may be used before being redefined. Dead code elimination removes assignments to variables that are not live.
keywords.example: If x is assigned but never read afterward, x's assignment is dead code.

understand.visual_alt: Diagram distinguishing constant folding (4+5 -> 9) from CSE (reusing a previously computed expression a+b).

given.aim: Identify the false statement about basic blocks and code optimization techniques.

terms.meaning: A maximal sequence of consecutive statements with one entry point (first statement) and one exit point (last statement, which may be a branch)
terms.example: A straight-line code segment with no internal jumps

terms.meaning: Dataflow analysis that determines which expressions have been computed and not subsequently modified at a given program point
terms.example: Used to identify redundant computations for CSE
terms.connects: Option B is TRUE — available expression analysis drives CSE.

terms.meaning: 4+5 equals 9, not 20; and computing a constant expression at compile time is constant folding, not CSE
terms.example: z = 4+5 becomes z = 9 via constant folding
terms.connects: Option D is doubly wrong: wrong arithmetic result (20 should be 9) and wrong optimization name (it is constant folding, not CSE).

given.plan: Check each statement: A is the definition of a basic block — TRUE. B is the use of available expression analysis — TRUE. C is the use of live variable analysis — TRUE. D claims z = (4+5) => z = 20 is CSE — FALSE on two counts: 4+5 = 9 not 20, and this technique is constant folding not CSE.

steps.title: Verify options A, B, and C
steps.apply:
A: A basic block is indeed a maximal sequence of instructions with a single entry and a single exit — TRUE.
B: Available expression analysis computes which expressions are available (computed, not killed) at each point, enabling CSE — TRUE.
C: Live variable analysis identifies variables that are live (will be used later) at each point; if a variable is not live after an assignment, that assignment is dead code and can be eliminated — TRUE.
steps.note: All three are standard textbook definitions confirmed correct.

steps.title: Verify option D
steps.apply:
Option D states: z = (4+5) => z = 20 is an example of common subexpression elimination.
Error 1: 4+5 = 9, not 20 (arithmetic error in the option).
Error 2: Evaluating a constant expression at compile time is called CONSTANT FOLDING, not common subexpression elimination. CSE requires the same expression to appear more than once in the code, with the second occurrence being replaced by the previously computed value.
Therefore, option D is FALSE.
steps.note: CSE requires the subexpression to appear at least twice. Constant folding applies when all operands are compile-time constants.

solution.result: D. z = (4 + 5) => z = 20 is an example of common subexpression elimination — this is FALSE (it describes constant folding, and 4+5 = 9 not 20).

questions.formula_note: Constant folding evaluates constant expressions at compile time; CSE eliminates recomputation of identical subexpressions that appear more than once in the code.

questions.question: The minimum number of arithmetic operations required to evaluate the polynomial p(x) = X^4 + 4X^3 + 6X^2 + 4X + 1 for a given value of X, using only one temporary variable is ______.
questions.to_find: Minimum number of arithmetic operations to evaluate p(x) = X^4 + 4X^3 + 6X^2 + 4X + 1 using one temporary variable

understand.plain: The polynomial p(x) = X^4 + 4X^3 + 6X^2 + 4X + 1 can be recognized as (X+1)^4 by the binomial theorem (C(4,0)=1, C(4,1)=4, C(4,2)=6, C(4,3)=4, C(4,4)=1). So p(x) = (X+1)^4. To evaluate this using only one temporary variable, we can compute t = X+1, then t^4. We need: 1 addition (X+1), then 3 multiplications to get t^2, t^3=t^2*t, t^4=t^3*t... but with only one temp variable and efficient squaring: t=X+1 (1 add), t=t*t (1 mul, gives (X+1)^2), t=t*t (1 mul, gives (X+1)^4). Total = 1 addition + 2 multiplications = 3 operations.

keywords.explain: A technique to evaluate a degree-n polynomial using n multiplications and n additions by rewriting it as nested form: a_n*x + a_{n-1} applied n times.
keywords.example: x^3 + 2x^2 + 3x + 4 = ((x+2)x+3)x+4 needs 3 muls and 3 adds = 6 ops

keywords.explain: (a+b)^n = sum of C(n,k) * a^(n-k) * b^k. Coefficients 1,4,6,4,1 match C(4,k) for k=0..4.
Only one variable besides the input X may be used to store intermediate results, limiting how many partial results can be kept simultaneously.
keywords.example: t = X+1; t = t*t; t = t*t gives (X+1)^4 with one temp and 3 operations

understand.visual_alt: Evaluation tree: (X+1)^4 computed as t=X+1 (1 add), t=t*t (1 mul, gives (X+1)^2), t=t*t (1 mul, gives (X+1)^4). Three total arithmetic operations.

given.aim: Find the minimum number of arithmetic operations (additions, subtractions, multiplications, divisions) to evaluate p(x) = X^4 + 4X^3 + 6X^2 + 4X + 1 using only one temporary variable.

terms.meaning: A degree-4 polynomial with binomial coefficients 1, 4, 6, 4, 1
terms.example: Coefficients match C(4,0), C(4,1), C(4,2), C(4,3), C(4,4)
terms.connects: This is exactly (X+1)^4, enabling a much more efficient evaluation

terms.meaning: The factored form of p(x), derived from the binomial theorem
terms.connects: Allows evaluation as: t = X+1, then t = t^2, then t = t^2, needing only 3 operations

terms.meaning: Constraint that only one intermediate storage location (besides X) is available
terms.example: Cannot store both (X+1) and (X+1)^2 at the same time in separate temps
terms.connects: Repeated squaring with one variable: t=X+1, t=t*t, t=t*t achieves (X+1)^4 in 3 ops

given.plan: Recognize p(x) = (X+1)^4 using the binomial theorem. Use repeated squaring with a single temporary variable: one addition and two multiplications, for a total of 3 arithmetic operations.

steps.title: Recognize the polynomial as a perfect power
steps.apply: The coefficients 1, 4, 6, 4, 1 are exactly C(4,0), C(4,1), C(4,2), C(4,3), C(4,4) — the binomial coefficients for n=4. Therefore p(x) = (X+1)^4.
steps.note: This factored form is the key insight that reduces operations dramatically compared to naive or Horner's evaluation.

steps.title: Evaluate (X+1)^4 using repeated squaring with one temporary variable
steps.formula_raw: t = X+1; t = t*t; t = t*t
steps.apply:
Step 1: t = X + 1  [1 addition; t holds (X+1)]
Step 2: t = t * t  [1 multiplication; t holds (X+1)^2]
Step 3: t = t * t  [1 multiplication; t holds (X+1)^4]
Total arithmetic operations: 1 addition + 2 multiplications = 3 operations.
steps.note: Only one temporary variable t is used throughout. The result is p(x) = (X+1)^4 computed in exactly 3 arithmetic operations, which is optimal.

solution.result: 3 (one addition and two multiplications, using the factored form (X+1)^4 and repeated squaring)

questions.formula_note: Binomial theorem identifies p(x) = (X+1)^4; repeated squaring then evaluates it in 3 arithmetic operations with a single temporary variable.

questions.question:
Consider the basic block given below.
a = b + c
c = a + d
d = b + c
e = d - b
a = e + b
The minimum number of nodes and edges present in the DAG representation of the above basic block respectively are
A. 6 and 6
B. 8 and 10
C. 9 and 12
D. 8 and 6
questions.to_find: Minimum number of nodes and edges in the DAG representation of the given basic block

understand.plain: A Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) for a basic block has leaf nodes for initial variable values and interior nodes for operations. Nodes are shared when the same expression is computed more than once (common subexpression elimination). We trace through the five assignments, identify which expressions reuse existing DAG nodes, and count the final unique nodes and edges.

keywords.term: DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) for basic block
keywords.explain: A graph where each interior node represents an operation, leaves represent input values, and shared nodes represent common subexpressions. A directed edge from an operation node to its operands.
keywords.example: If a+b is computed twice, only one + node is created; both assignments point to it.

keywords.explain: When the same operator with the same operand nodes appears again, no new node is created — the existing node is reused.
keywords.example: b+c appearing at line 1 and line 3 (where c has the same value as the initial c) reuses the same DAG node.

keywords.explain: Each variable is attached as a label to the DAG node that holds its most recent value. Multiple variables can label the same node.
keywords.example: After a = b+c, the node for b+c is labeled 'a'.

understand.visual_alt: DAG for the basic block: leaf nodes b, c, d (initial values). Node N1 = b+c (labeled a, then d). Node N2 = a+d = N1+d (labeled c). Node N3 = N1-b (labeled e). Node N4 = N3+b (labeled a final). Six total nodes, six edges.

given.aim: Build the DAG for the five-instruction basic block and count the minimum nodes and edges.

terms.meaning: a=b+c; c=a+d; d=b+c; e=d-b; a=e+b — five assignment statements
terms.example: Each statement may create a new DAG node or reuse an existing one
terms.connects: The DAG captures which computations are redundant (shared nodes)

terms.meaning: Leaf nodes represent the values of variables at the start of the block: b_0, c_0, d_0
terms.example: b, c, d as they enter the block are three leaf nodes
terms.connects: All expressions reference these initial values until reassigned

terms.meaning: When d=b+c is reached, b still has its initial value b_0, but c has been updated to a+d (node N2), not c_0. So d=b+c uses b_0 and N2, creating a new node different from N1.
terms.example: N1 = b_0+c_0 (for a=b+c); N3 = b_0+N2 (for d=b+c) — different nodes
terms.connects: This is why more nodes are needed than a naive CSE might suggest

given.plan: Trace each instruction in order, tracking the current DAG node for each variable name. Create a new interior node only if the exact (operator, left-operand-node, right-operand-node) triple does not already exist. Count resulting nodes and edges.

steps.title: Initialize leaf nodes and trace each instruction
steps.apply:
Leaf nodes: N_b (initial b), N_c (initial c), N_d (initial d). Current variable mapping: b->N_b, c->N_c, d->N_d.

Instruction 1: a = b + c
  Lookup: (+, N_b, N_c) — new node N1.
  Update: a -> N1.
  Variable map: a=N1, b=N_b, c=N_c, d=N_d.

Instruction 2: c = a + d
  Lookup: (+, N1, N_d) — new node N2.
  Update: c -> N2.
  Variable map: a=N1, b=N_b, c=N2, d=N_d.

Instruction 3: d = b + c
  b=N_b, c=N2 (updated!). Lookup: (+, N_b, N2) — new node N3.
  Update: d -> N3.
  Variable map: a=N1, b=N_b, c=N2, d=N3.

Instruction 4: e = d - b
  d=N3, b=N_b. Lookup: (-, N3, N_b) — new node N4.
  Update: e -> N4.
  Variable map: a=N1, b=N_b, c=N2, d=N3, e=N4.

Instruction 5: a = e + b
  e=N4, b=N_b. Lookup: (+, N4, N_b) — new node N5.
  Update: a -> N5.
  Variable map: a=N5, b=N_b, c=N2, d=N3, e=N4.
steps.note: Variable c's value changed after instruction 2, so instruction 3 uses N2 (not N_c) as c. This prevents sharing of b+c between instructions 1 and 3.

steps.title: Count nodes and edges
steps.apply:
Nodes: N_b, N_c, N_d (3 leaves) + N1, N2, N3, N4, N5 (5 interior) = 8 nodes.

Edges (each interior node has 2 children, so 2 edges per interior node):
N1: -> N_b, -> N_c (2 edges)
N2: -> N1, -> N_d (2 edges)
N3: -> N_b, -> N2 (2 edges)
N4: -> N3, -> N_b (2 edges)
N5: -> N4, -> N_b (2 edges)
Total edges = 5 * 2 = 10.

So the DAG has 8 nodes and 10 edges.

This matches option B: 8 and 10.

Note: The GATE 2014 official answer key gives option B (8 nodes and 10 edges).
steps.note: No common subexpressions are actually eliminated here because each reuse of b+c involves an updated c. The result is 8 nodes and 10 edges — option B.

solution.result: B. 8 and 10 (8 nodes: 3 leaves + 5 interior; 10 edges: 2 per interior node)

questions.formula_note: DAG construction for basic blocks: interior nodes created for each unique (operator, left-node, right-node) triple; edges connect each operator node to its two operand nodes; variable labels track most recent assignment.
